title: Getting random 403 Forbidden errors on the dashboard
body: I keep getting 403 errors when I try to access the TaskFlow dashboard. It's not every time — sometimes it loads fine, sometimes I get a 403 page. It seems totally random. My permissions haven't changed and other people on my team say they're seeing it too. Started happening a couple days ago.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for reporting this — intermittent 403s are frustrating, especially when they seem random.

To help us dig into this: can you walk me through what a typical session looks like when this happens? For example, are you freshly logging in when you hit the 403, or does it happen mid-session while you're already working? And when it does load fine, is there anything different about those times — like time of day, which browser you're using, or whether you navigated there directly vs. from another page?

Also, you mentioned this started a couple days ago — do you recall if anything changed around that time? A new deployment, a team membership change, anything like that?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
It happens both ways — sometimes I get the 403 right when I first load the dashboard, other times I'll be working and click to a different page and get it. There's no real pattern I can see with time of day or browser (I just use Chrome). When it works, it works totally fine, and then the next click might give me a 403. It's like a coin flip almost, maybe slightly less frequent than that.

As for what changed — I was actually assigned a new role recently, the 'analyst' role. That was around the same time this started, now that you mention it. I kind of assumed it was just network issues on our end though. The other people on my team who are seeing it were also moved to the analyst role I think.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That's a really helpful detail — the timing with the analyst role change lines up well. That's worth investigating for sure.

Here's what's curious though: if it were just a missing permission on the analyst role, you'd expect it to fail *every* time, not intermittently. The coin-flip behavior suggests something more nuanced.

Can you tell me a bit more about what happens when you get the 403? For instance:
- When you hit the 403 and then refresh the same page, does it sometimes load successfully on the retry? Or once you get a 403, are you stuck until you do something specific (like logging out and back in)?
- Is it always the dashboard page specifically that returns the 403, or does it happen on other pages too? If other pages, are there specific ones that seem more prone to it?

I'm trying to understand whether the 403 is "sticky" once it happens or whether it truly flips back and forth — that distinction could point us toward the right layer of the system to investigate.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
Yeah, if I hit a 403 and just refresh the same page, it usually comes back fine after one or two refreshes. It's definitely not sticky — it flips back and forth randomly. I don't need to log out or anything, just refreshing works eventually.

And it's not just the dashboard — it happens on pretty much any page. I'll click into a project view and get a 403, refresh, and it loads. Or I'll go to the reports section and same thing. I don't think any specific page is worse than others, it just seems like any request has a chance of getting the 403.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks, the non-sticky, any-page behavior is really telling. Here's what I'm starting to piece together: the fact that it's truly random per-request (not per-session or per-page), and that a simple refresh can fix it, strongly suggests that your requests are landing on different servers behind a load balancer — and some of those servers aren't recognizing the analyst role's permissions correctly. The role change timing fits perfectly with that.

One more thing that would help us narrow this down for the developers: when you get the 403, what does the error page actually look like? Is it a TaskFlow-branded error page (with TaskFlow's logo or styling), or is it a plain/generic "403 Forbidden" page? And if you're comfortable checking — could you open your browser's developer tools (F12 → Network tab), reproduce the 403, and tell us what the response body looks like? Specifically, whether it returns any JSON (like an error code or message) or just an HTML page. That would tell us exactly which layer of the system is rejecting the request.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
It's a TaskFlow-branded error page — it has the logo and everything, with a message like 'You don't have permission to access this resource' or something along that lines. It's not a plain browser error page.

I tried the developer tools thing but I'm not super familiar with it. I opened the Network tab and refreshed until I got the 403. It looks like the response is JSON — I can see something like an error message and a 403 status code. I didn't see anything super specific in there beyond that though, sorry. If you need me to look for something particular I can try again.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Intermittent 403 errors for users with 'analyst' role — likely inconsistent permission state across app server instances","problem":"Users assigned the 'analyst' role receive random 403 Forbidden errors on any page in TaskFlow. The errors are non-sticky (a simple page refresh resolves them) and occur on roughly 30-50% of requests. Multiple users who were recently assigned the analyst role are affected. The 403 is returned as a TaskFlow-branded error page with a JSON response body, confirming the rejection happens at the application layer, not infrastructure.","root_cause_hypothesis":"The application runs behind a load balancer with multiple server instances. When the 'analyst' role was created or assigned, its permissions were not consistently propagated to all instances — likely due to a stale permissions cache, an incomplete role-permission configuration deployment, or a cache invalidation failure. Requests that land on an instance with the updated permissions succeed; requests routed to an instance with stale or missing analyst role mappings return 403.","reproduction_steps":["Log in as a user with the 'analyst' role","Navigate to any page in TaskFlow (dashboard, project view, reports, etc.)","Repeat navigation/refreshes — within a few attempts, a 403 Forbidden page should appear","Refresh the same page — it should load successfully within 1-2 retries"],"environment":"TaskFlow web application accessed via Chrome. Multiple app server instances behind a load balancer. Issue began approximately 2 days before report, coinciding with users being assigned the new 'analyst' role.","severity":"high","impact":"All users with the 'analyst' role are affected. The issue disrupts normal workflow on every page, requiring frequent refreshes. Multiple team members are impacted. While users can work around it by refreshing, the experience is degrading and the 403 rate (~30-50%) is high enough to significantly impair productivity.","recommended_fix":"1. Check the permissions/role cache invalidation mechanism across app server instances — verify that all instances have the analyst role's permissions loaded. 2. Inspect whether a recent deployment or role configuration change was only partially rolled out. 3. If using in-memory permission caching, add a cache invalidation or refresh mechanism triggered on role changes. 4. As an immediate mitigation, restart all app server instances to force a fresh permission load, or manually clear the permissions cache on all nodes.","proposed_test_case":"Deploy a new role with specific page permissions, assign it to a test user, and verify that 100% of requests across all server instances return 200 (not intermittent 403s). The test should make at least 50 sequential requests to confirm consistent behavior across all load-balanced instances.","information_gaps":["Whether users who were NOT assigned the analyst role experience any 403 errors (team can verify internally)","Exact number of app server instances and load balancer configuration","Whether the analyst role was newly created or is a pre-existing role with recently modified permissions","Specific fields in the JSON 403 response (e.g., error codes that might identify the authorization subsystem)"]}
